coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional metal–organic composites with controlled structures and properties are attractive for smart optoelectronic, drug delivery, cancer therapy, clean energy, and environmental applications. Here we develop lightweight, porous metal-graphene composites with uniformly dispersed nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) using reactive non-thermal microplasmas under ambient conditions. Synergy of NGQDs and AuNPs accelerates the polymer cross-linking during synthesis and ensures the requisite properties for simultaneous water monitoring and purification including reliable photoluminescence for heavy metal detection, as well as high adsorption capacity and fast catalytic degradation of organic pollutants. Large-area hierarchical composites with tunable porosity and self-healing ability can be fabricated by the fast and scalable plasma process avoiding toxic chemicals and high temperatures. The composite achieves 120 g g−1 water purification within 60 min at 0.13 mM 4-nitrophenol concentration and is able to monitor the water quality against Hg2+ with the detection limit of 2.2 μM, producing 2880 L kg−1 freshwater per day. Besides, almost 100 % of recalcitrant dye, remazol brilliant blue R, with the concentration of 20 ppm can be adsorbed by the composite in 120 min. Our work provides new insights into design and production of high-performance graphene-metal composites for emerging applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of NGQDs, AuNPs, and composites. (a) Absorption spectra of the synthesized NGQDs and AuNPs using microplasma at 8 mA discharge current for 20 min. (b) Photographs of the chitosan control (denoted as CS) without plasma treatment and plasma-treated solutions with different gold salt ratios. (c) PL spectra with the highest intensity of the colloidal NGQDs and AuNPs. (d) Photographs of the fabricated hydrogels cross-linked with microplasma at 8 mA discharge current for 20 min.

TEM analysis of the synthesized NGQDs and AuNPs. Low magnification TEM images of (a) NGQDs (ratio 0), and AuNPs with gold salt to chitosan ratios of (b) 0.82, (c) 1.64, and (d) 2.45. (e, g) High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images and (f, h) the corresponding inverse FFT images of NGQD and AuNP, respectively. Inset shows the FFT image. The scale bar shown is 5 nm.

Mechanical properties of the plasma cross-linked composites. (a) Compression test and (b) water storage measurements of the fabricated composite with different gold salt to chitosan ratios. Inset: photographs of control chitosan, ratio 0, 0.82, 1.64, and 2.45 composite sponges.

Morphological structures of the plasma-cross-linked sponges. SEM images of (a) pristine chitosan sponge, (b) ratio 0, (c) ratio 0.82, (d) ratio 1.64, and (e) ratio 2.45 composite sponges cross-linked using microplasma at 8 mA discharge current for 20 min. The SEM images shows the (1) morphological and (2) pore structures.

Water monitoring and purification enabled by NGQD/AuNP composites. Heavy metal ions sensing with NGQD composites showing (a) metal ion selectivity, and (b) PL sensing performance of NGQD composites for Hg2+. Dye removal application of NGQD/AuNP composites displaying (c) dye selectivity test, and (d) adsorption isotherm of RBBR on NGQD composites. Catalytic reduction of 4-NP depicting (e) first-order reaction kinetic, and (f) temperature effect on the conversion of 4-NP in the presence of Sample C. Inset shows the Arrhenius plot to estimate the activation energy of Sample C for catalytic reduction of 4-NP.

Cr(VI) sensing with colloidal NGQDs. (a) Heavy metal ion selectivity test of colloidal NGQDs. (b) PL spectra of NGQD solution containing Cr(VI) at different concentrations. (c) PL sensing performance of colloidal NGQDs for Cr(VI).

Microplasma engineering of 3D porous graphene-metal composites for water monitoring and purification. (a) “From Nanoscale to Macroscale” strategy based on microplasma technologies to fabricate an organic-metal composite comprising of NGQDs/AuNPs and chitosan as nanocross-linkers and polymeric matrix, respectively. (b) The engineered NGQD/AuNP composites show controlled 3D porous structures, stable solid-state PL properties, and superior mechanical stabilities. (c) The engineered NGQD/AuNP composites exhibit remarkable water treatment applications including heavy metal ion sensing, toxic dye adsorption, and catalytic reduction of organic pollutant.

Summary of the two-step plasma synthesis conditions of composites. Au salt used is hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III) trihydrate and chitosan concentration is 62.50 µM. The plasma current is 8 mA and process duration is 20 min.

head.ce:title: Plasma-bioresource-derived multifunctional porous NGQD/AuNP nanocomposites for water monitoring and purification

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional metal–organic composites with controlled structures and properties are attractive for smart optoelectronic, drug delivery, cancer therapy, clean energy, and environmental applications. Here we develop lightweight, porous metal-graphene composites with uniformly dispersed nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) using reactive non-thermal microplasmas under ambient conditions. Synergy of NGQDs and AuNPs accelerates the polymer cross-linking during synthesis and ensures the requisite properties for simultaneous water monitoring and purification including reliable photoluminescence for heavy metal detection, as well as high adsorption capacity and fast catalytic degradation of organic pollutants. Large-area hierarchical composites with tunable porosity and self-healing ability can be fabricated by the fast and scalable plasma process avoiding toxic chemicals and high temperatures. The composite achieves 120 g g−1 water purification within 60 min at 0.13 mM 4-nitrophenol concentration and is able to monitor the water quality against Hg2+ with the detection limit of 2.2 μM, producing 2880 L kg−1 freshwater per day. Besides, almost 100 % of recalcitrant dye, remazol brilliant blue R, with the concentration of 20 ppm can be adsorbed by the composite in 120 min. Our work provides new insights into design and production of high-performance graphene-metal composites for emerging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The very well-known Minamata disease caused by severe mercury poisoning has brought the world to pay a very close attention to the water quality particularly against toxic heavy metal ions, like Hg2+ and Cr(VI) due to their fatality to human body, brain, and nervous system [1,2]. Therefore, rapid detection considered to be one important final step in the quality control of water treatment process to ensure the water safety for further usage, is vital in detecting Hg2+ and Cr(VI) which are colorless and highly soluble in water, making their distinction very difficult if not impossible. Organic dyes are other major sources of water pollution that mainly come from textile industries, which are extremely harmful for aquatic environment due to their inherent toxicity, carcinogenicity, recalcitrant characteristic, and mutagenicity [3,4]. Removal of such dyes in a cost-efficient, yet effective and simple way via adsorption process is demanded so that the dyes can be reused after the desorption process, thereby recovering their material cost and increasing the overall environmental friendliness and sustainability of the process. Another type of pollutants involves nitroarene family considered as “Priority Pollutants” by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) owing to their severe toxicity, causing liver, kidney, and central nervous system damages to human [5]. Conversion of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) by catalytic reduction into a valuable pharmaceutical intermediate, 4-aminophenol (4-AP), is one of the most common methods of water treatment [6].

Metal-organic composites with controlled structures and properties are actively pursued for diverse applications such as optoelectronics, sensing, imaging, drug delivery, cancer therapy, energy generation and storage, environmental monitoring and purification, and many others [7–11]. Among those applications, stable 3D architectural multifunctional metal–organic composites with high efficacy in water treatment are highly desired for next-generation clean and sustainable technologies. Despite the overwhelming number of single-function composite materials [12–17], those composites that can be used for multifunctional water treatment, including pollutant detection, capture, and degradation are extremely rare. Moreover, the synthesis of multifunctional composites derived from renewable and naturally available resources using a single environment-friendly process is still not available. Current synthesis methods of metal–organic composites face major problems including long preparation time, laborious step, toxic chemicals, harsh solvent, high temperature, uncontrolled structures and properties [18,19]. For example, Cai et al. fabricated nanocomposites based on silver nanoparticles and black fungus polysaccharide using DMSO as solvent with continuous stirring for 6 h at 100 °C [17]. Zhang et al. synthesized ZnO/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites via hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 24 h [16].

Inspired by the marine sponge with the properties of smart sensory system for monitoring environmental stimuli, large surface area created by porous structures and enzymatic activity for water purification, here we report a nanotechnology-enhanced rational design of catalytically active and environmentally-responsive bioresource-derived 3D porous metal–organic composites. These composites contain nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and are synthesized using non-thermal microplasmas under ambient conditions. GQD represents a unique zero-dimensional graphene derivative, which exhibits stable photoluminescence (PL) properties arising from the quantum confinement and edge effects. PL is a radiative relaxation process of an electron from the excited state to the ground state upon photon excitation [20]. Featuring biocompatibility, nontoxicity, chemical- and photo-stability, high water solubility, low particle agglomeration, and ease of synthesis and functionalization from naturally available bioresource [21,22], GQDs are more attractive to be applied and integrated into nanocomposite systems for chemical and biological sensing compared with graphene, graphene oxide, and some other related materials [1,23–26]. Furthermore, functionalization of GQDs via heteroatom doping, e.g., nitrogen (N), can enhance the PL quantum yield (PLQY), tune the surface polarity, and modulate the optical and electronic properties [27], resulting in improved sensing [28], imaging [29], catalysis [30,31], and optoelectronic device performances [32,33]. On the other hand, AuNPs have shown specific catalytic properties coupled with good biocompatibility, low toxicity, as well as excellent chemical inertness and better stability compared with other metal NPs [34,35], beneficial for both medical and environmental applications [36,37].

Non-thermal plasma processing is effective to fabricate composites with a long-term stability due to improved NPs dispersion within the polymeric matrix [38]. The plasma-induced reactive species, including electrons, radicals, ions, and UV photons are very effective for nanomaterial syntheses [1,39–43] and cross-linking of polymeric composites at ambient conditions [44]. Several reports have shown microplasma-based syntheses of metal NPs [40,45], GQDs [41], SiQDs [43], and 2D materials [46], yet plasma-derived GQD-metal NP composites are scarcely investigated.

Here we report rapid and environmental-friendly plasma engineering of lightweight and porous 3D NGQD/AuNP composites from chitosan using non-thermal microplasmas at ambient conditions without toxic chemicals and high temperature treatment, thereby alleviating the current major problem of metal–organic composite syntheses [16,17]. The highly reactive species generated by the plasma are used to simultaneously synthesize both NGQDs and AuNPs from chitosan containing gold salt (Scheme 1
a). The unique thermal non-equilibrium properties of microplasmas enable rapid conversion of precursors into metal-graphene composites in a short time. Moreover, synergistic effect of the NGQDs and AuNPs as the nanocross-linkers can accelerate the cross-linking of chitosan to form a 3D porous composite without the need of toxic chemicals and high-temperature treatment. The engineered composites exhibit multifunctional properties including stable solid-state PL emission, tunable surface functionalities, catalytic ability, high porosity, and mechanical robustness (Scheme 1 b), having potential for water treatments including selective PL detection of Hg2+, remarkable adsorption of remazol brilliant blue R (RBBR), and catalytic reduction of 4-NP (Scheme 1 c). It is envisaged that advanced functional materials capable to “actively hunt” for pollutants by detecting, capturing, and converting/reducing them, are crucial for the next step in the environmental applications; the presently available state-of-the-art nanocomposites lack this ability [12–17]. Furthermore, our method can also yield colloidal NGQDs used for Cr(VI) detection in solution, ranging from 1 to 100 μM with a 61 nM limit of detection (LoD). The as-prepared composites could achieve 120 g g−1 water purification within 60 min at 0.13 mM 4-NP concentration and produce 2880 L kg−1 freshwater per day. Importantly, complete redundance of vacuum conditions makes plasma-based methods amenable for industrial settings and scales. Our work provides not only a general method to engineer organic-metal composites with defined structures and properties, but also a new insight into their rational design for emerging applications.

Chitosan (low molecular weight, 50 – 190 kDa, CAS: 9012–76-4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥98 %, CAS: 1310–73-2) pellet, rhodamine B (RB, ∼95 %, CAS: 81–88-9), cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, ≥99 %, CAS: 10022–68-1), lead(II) nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, ACS reagent ≥ 99 %, CAS: 10099–74-8), and copper(II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O, CAS: 10125–13-0), and iron(III) chloride (FeCl3, reagent grade 97 %, CAS: 7705–08-0) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Co., ltd. Acetic acid glacial (≥99.7 %, CAS: 64–19-7) was received from Fischer Scientific. Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III) trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, 99.99 %, CAS: 16961–25-4), methylene blue (MB, CAS: 122965–43-9), congo red (CR, CAS: 573–58-0), and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP, 99 %, CAS: 100–02-7) were obtained from Alfa Aesar. Sodium borohydride (98+%, CAS: 16940–66-2), crystal violet (CV, CAS: 548–62-9), remazol brilliant blue R (RBBR, CAS: 2580–78-1), methyl orange (MO, CAS: 547–58-0), mercury(II) nitrate monohydrate (Hg(NO3)2·H2O, 98+%, CAS: 7783–34-8), sodium arsenite (NaAsO2, 98+%, CAS: 7784–46-5) and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, 99.5 %, CAS: 7778–50-9) were received from Acros Organics. Pt foil (99.95 %, 20 mm × 20 mm × 0.1 mm) was purchased from Guv Team International Co., ltd., Taiwan. PTFE membrane filter (M−PTFE047N022O, 47 mm diameter, 0.22 µm pore size) was obtained from Finetech Research and Innovation Co., Taiwan., ltd.

The NGQDs and AuNPs were synthesized using a direct current (DC) argon (Ar) microplasma electrochemical reactor operated at ambient conditions. The electrochemical reactor was equipped with a 25 sccm (standard cubic centimeter per minute) of Ar gas flowing through a hollow capillary cathode tube (inner diameter: 178 μm) and a Pt foil (1 × 1 cm2) was used as the anode. The distance between the cathode tube and the anode foil was adjusted to 2.5 cm. 75 mg of chitosan was dissolved in 5 mL aqueous solution containing 100 mM acetic acid and further diluted by deionized (DI) water to obtain 10 mL of chitosan precursor solution with the concentration of 62.50 μM. To initiate the reaction, around two third of the foil was immersed deep into the chitosan electrolyte solution, and the plasma was brought into contact with the liquid surface and maintained by a DC power supply connected to a ballast resistor of 150 kΩ. The reaction was carried out in current limiting mode at 8 mA discharge current for 20 min to synthesize NGQDs at ambient conditions. For the production of AuNPs, similar procedure was conducted to obtain the initial chitosan precursor solution. Afterwards, various amount of HAuCl4 was added into the 62.50 μM of chitosan solution to yield three different molar ratios of HAuCl4 to chitosan (i.e. ratio 0.82, 1.64, and 2.45), and followed by treating the resulted mixtures with microplasma at 8 mA discharge current for 20 min at ambient conditions. The previously synthesized pristine colloidal NGQD was labelled as ratio 0 for the convenient of sample name comparison with the AuNPs. 1 M of NaOH was then added dropwise to the plasma treated solution of NGQDs/AuNPs to convert the unreacted chitosan into gel with pH between 8 and 9. The formed gel was separated from the liquid solution by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 5 min. The separated gel was then transferred into an autoclave Teflon mould (3 cm × 1 cm × 0.5 cm) for shaping, while the separated liquid solution was moved into a reaction dish. Once the gel had taken the rectangle form of the mould, the gel was taken out and immersed into the separated liquid medium in the reaction dish, and followed by microplasma treatment at 8 mA discharge current for 20 min to cross-link the hydrogel at ambient conditions. The immersed hydrogel was placed in between the Pt foil anode and plasma cathode during the cross-linking process. Afterwards, the hydrogel was taken out and rinsed with DI water before storing at 4 °C for further usage, while the liquid solution containing NGQDs was moved into a sample vial and also stored in 4 °C for further study. To further modify the structures of the prepared composites, the prepared composites were frozen at −20 °C for 18 h and followed by thawing at room temperature for 6 h. This freeze–thaw process was repeated for 3 times and the finally obtained composite sponge was stored in a desicator for further usage.

Acetone was added into the synthesized colloidal NGQDs with a volume ratio of 2:1 of that acetone and NGQD solution to precipitate the chitosan which was not converted into the gel. Then, the suspension was subjected to vacuum filtration to remove the precipitate. The obtained solution was further evaporated using a rotary evaporator equipped with a vacuum controller (Eyela, Japan) at a low pressure of ∼ 0.5 – 5 kPa and a constant temperature of −10 °C maintained by a continuous cooling system. During the evaporation process, the sample vial containing the NGQD solution was partially immersed in water bath at 25 °C. Once all of the solution had been evaporated, a little amount of water was added into the sample vial to disperse the NGQDs. The NGQDs was further dialyzed against DI water for 24 h to remove the formed salt or buffer during the conversion of unreacted chitosan into gel by the addition of NaOH. After dialysis, the NGQD solution was once again evaporated using rotary evaporator. After evaporating all of the solution, a little amount of ethanol was added to disperse the NGQDs stuck to the sample vial wall. Finally, the suspension was dried once again using a rotary evaporator and fine NGQD powder was obtained after drying overnight in a chamber filled with silica gel.

PL measurements of all liquid and solid samples were performed at room temperature using a commercial spectrometer (Horiba JobinYvon Nanolog-3 spectrofluorometer) equipped with an InGaAs NIR detector and a 20 nm bandpass for both emission and excitation. The PL spectra obtained were scaled according to the measured excitation power. Absorbance spectra of all materials were recorded using a JASCO V676 absorbance spectrophotometer with a matched pair of 1 cm path length quartz cuvettes. DI water was used as baseline reference for every spectral measurement. Raman measurement was recorded using a LabRAM HR Evolution spectrometer (Horiba) equipped with a He-Cd laser (λ = 325 nm). The spectral resolution was 0.5–1.0 cm−1. The purified NGQDs were drop-casted on Si wafers and dried on a hotplate at 35 °C in ambient conditions. The laser power was adjusted and maintained at 0.1 mW to avoid sample damages by the laser. A silicon wafer with a Raman shifts of 520 cm−1 peak was used to calibrate the Raman spectrometer before the measurement. TEM measurements were performed using a field emission gun TEM (JEOL JEM-2100F) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. TEM samples were prepared from solution and gel dry-casting of the NGQD colloidal solution and AuNP hydrogel, respectively, onto formvar/carbon-coated copper grids (300 mesh, EM Resolutions ltd.). Digital Micrograph 3.7 software was utilized to obtain the particle size distributions and lattice spacing of the synthesized NGQDs and AuNPs.

25 mg of NGQD/AuNP composite was softened by 20 mL of DI water submersion to allow water penetration into the composite. The composite was then blotted on a filter paper and squeezed to remove the water. Once the composite had been softened and the water had been squeezed out of the composite, it was immersed in a 5 mL of dye solution with pre-determined concentration. During the dye adsorption process, the solution was magnetically stirred at 180 rpm and around 3 mL of the solution was taken out for every 10 min to measure the absorbance using UV–vis spectrophotometer. During the dye selectivity adsorption experiment, the dye concentration used was 20 ppm and the adsorption was conducted for 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic degradation of 4-NP with NGQD/AuNP composites
ce:section.ce:para: Similar procedure was conducted by soaking a 25 mg of NGQD/AuNP sponge in 20 mL of DI water first and followed by blotting on a filter paper and squeezing the water out of the composite. The composite was put in a sample vial containing a mixture of 0.4 mL of 1 mM 4-NP, 1.3 mL of DI water, and 1.3 mL of 0.1 M NaBH4. During the catalytic degradation of 4-NP, the mixture was magnetically stirred at 180 rpm and the absorption was measured by taking 3 mL of the solution for every 5 min using UV–vis spectrophotometer. The catalytic degradations using different composites were conducted for 1 h and the results were compared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion detection using NGQD/AuNP composites
ce:section.ce:para: The NGQD composite was cut into pieces using a surgical knife with a dimension of 1 cm × 0.3 cm × 0.5 cm for each. The cut NGQD composite was let to fully absorb water by soaking in 20 mL of DI water (labelled wet sponges). The PL of the wet sponge was measured using a solid sample stage of PL spectrophotometer operated in a front face (FF) mode with 45°. The measured PL intensity of the wet NGQD sponge was referred as blank and labelled as I0 . Afterwards, the wet sponge was blotted on a filter paper and squeezed to remove the water out of the sponge. 4 mL of different metal ion solutions were prepared by dissolving a certain amount of various metal salts in DI water to yield a 50 μM of metal ion concentration. One piece of NGQD sponge which had the water been squeezed out beforehand was immersed in a metal ion solution for 2 min. Then, the NGQD sponge was taken out and the PL intensity was measured which was referred as sample and labelled as I. Once the NGQD sponge had shown a selectivity toward a certain metal ion, the concentration of the corresponding metal ion was varied and followed with the same experimental procedure to obtain the PL intensity of the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion detection using NGQD solution
ce:section.ce:para: A NGQD solution with a concentration of 300 μg mL−1 was prepared by dispersing the NGQD powder in a DI water. 1 mL of NGQD solution was moved into a quartz cuvette and mixed with 1.5 mL of 100 μM metal ion solution and 0.5 mL of DI water. The final concentrations of the NGQDs and metal ions were 100 μg mL−1 and 50 μM, respectively. The mixture was incubated for 3 min before measuring the PL intensity using PL spectrophotometer, labelled as I. Blank measurement was also conducted by measuring the PL intensity of NGQD solution with the absence of metal ions (1 mL NGQD solution and 2 mL of DI water), labelled as I0 . All of the emission was collected in a right angle (RA) mode (90° relative to the excitation beam). Once the NGQD solution had demonstrated a selectivity toward a certain metal ion, the concentration of the corresponding metal ion was changed and the PL intensity was measured.

Figure S1 shows the schematic of NGQD/AuNP composite synthesis using a two-step microplasma process. The details can be found in the experimental section. First, the NGQDs and AuNPs were synthesized using a 20 min plasma treatment on a mixture solution of hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III) trihydrate and chitosan as the AuNP and NGQD precursors as well as composite matrix, respectively. Noted that chitosan also functions as the capping agent to prevent Au particle agglomeration during the growth. Four different molar ratios of gold salt to chitosan were used to tune the structures of AuNP and NGQD in the composites in this work, including ratio 0, 0.82, 1.64, and 2.45. Since ratio 0 contains no gold salt, only NGQDs were formed by the plasma. Afterwards, 1 M NaOH was added to neutralize the plasma-treated NGQDs/AuNPs suspension. Then an additional 20 min plasma treatment was performed to fabricate the composites by cross-linking of residual chitosan. Table 1 summarized the synthesis conditions of composites. It is imperative to note that the amount of gold salt used was very low with the highest amount used was 0.60 mg only for one batch of synthesis, suggesting that the precursor cost is not a significant issue in our experiments.

The existence of NGQDs and AuNPs in the plasma treated solution can be confirmed using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 1
a). An absorption peak at 279 nm and a tail around 350 – 350 nm respectively correspond to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of CC and CO/CN bonds in NGQD. The existence of N-doping in the carbon lattice was confirmed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements in different configurations (details can be seen in supporting information S2 and Figure S2 ). In addition, the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band of AuNPs is monitored around 480 – 650 nm that peaks at ∼ 520 nm. As the ratio of gold precursor increases, the SPR band becomes more prominent (Fig. 1 a inset) and more reddish solution under white light (Fig. 1 b), suggesting higher concentration of AuNPs. Noted that there is a slight increment in the absorption peak of NGQDs with the higher ratio of gold salt which could be caused by the formation of NGQD-AuNP complex coordination (denoted as NGQDs/AuNPs) (see Fig. 1 a). PL spectroscopy was performed to investigate PL properties of the plasma-synthesized NGQDs/AuNPs. Figure S3 depicts the PL maps with excitation-dependent emission properties centered at 370 and 451 nm of that excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively. The highest intensity PL spectra of the colloidal NGQDs/AuNPs are additionally summarized in Fig. 1 c. The PL intensity becomes weaker when the concentration of AuNPs increases which might be due to the complexation-induced PL quenching of NGQDs by AuNPs, as shown in Fig. 1 a. Importantly, the 3D network within the NGQD/AuNP hydrogels was successfully established, producing robust hydrogels without any structural collapse, as presented in Fig. 1 d. The hydrogels are formed due to the deprotonation of amine side chain of chitosan during the neutralization process, reducing the solubility of chitosan that eventually leads to gelation process and simultaneous entrapment of NGQDs/AuNPs. The amount of NGQDs in the composites was estimated to be 16.0 ± 2.2 % based on UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, while the amount of AuNPs was in the range of 0.1 – 0.3 % for the gold salt to chitosan ratio 0.82 – 2.45 based on the elemental stoichiometry analysis. For one hour plasma synthesis, about 100 mg of lightweight composites can be obtained. We exploited the synergistic effect of the NGQDs/AuNPs within the chitosan as nanocross-linkers and the plasma-generated reactive species to promote a facile, green, sustainable, and rapid cross-linking of NGQD/AuNP composites at ambient conditions. Further implementation of microplasma array coupled with continuous process is a viable way towards the fabrication of composites at industry-relevant scales [47,48].

To reveal the morphologies of the NGQDs and AuNPs in the composites, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed (Fig. 2
a-d). The TEM image of the sample A was obtained from the pristine NGQD specimen (Fig. 2 a). Fig. 2 b-d show the uniform distribution of the AuNPs in the composites without any apparent particle aggregation. The average particle size of each sample can be estimated from the TEM images. As the ratio of the gold salt increases, the average particle size also increases from 3.7 to 9.4 nm ( Figure S4 ). This result suggests that more reduction and nucleation of gold ions, yielding larger AuNPs with increasing gold salt ratio. High-resolution TEM measurements were further performed to investigate the crystalline structures of the synthesized NGQDs (Fig. 2 e) and AuNPs (Fig. 2 g). The FFT images in Fig. 2 f & 2 h inset reveal the crystalline structures of both NGQDs and AuNPs, respectively. By conducting an inverse FFT, one can clearly determine the lattice spacing of the NGQDs and AuNPs. As a result, the NGQD reveals a lattice spacing of 0.215 nm corresponds to the (100) plane of graphene structure (Fig. 2 f) [41], while Fig. 2 h indicates a lattice spacing of 0.256 nm which can be assigned to the (111) plane of gold [49]. In brief, the direct TEM measurement on the composite film specimen enables the analysis of particle distributions within the matrices, providing strong evidence of the uniformly-dispersed NGQDs and AuNPs across the whole composite systems. Furthermore, the observable lattice fringe and crystallinity structure of both NGQDs and AuNPs in the TEM images infer that the synthesized materials contain high purity and free of any residual carbon from chitosan dissociation.

To study the effect of NGQDs/AuNPs as nanocross-linkers on the mechanical properties of the composites, NGQD and chitosan hydrogels were taken as representatives to assess the mechanical properties. Fig. S5 a-b compares the mechanical robustness of the pristine chitosan (white) and NGQD hydrogels (yellow). Interestingly, upon compression with 100 g of loading, the chitosan hydrogel was deflated (Fig. S5 a top), while NGQD hydrogel was able to withstand the load and preserved its initial form (Fig. S5 b top). The mechanical robustness of the NGQD hydrogel over chitosan was further elaborated by lifting the samples up after the compression process. Fig. S5 a bottom displays the structurally collapsed chitosan hydrogel while on the contrary, NGQD hydrogel shows mechanical stability after compression, as can be seen in Fig. S5 b bottom and Video S1 . We assume that the improved mechanical stability of NGQD hydrogel is attributed to the NGQD-enhanced cross-linking and polymerization of chitosan under plasma treatment, induced by the various covalent and non-covalent interactions between the functional groups and side chains of NGQDs and chitosan, respectively [7,50,51]. Since the NGQDs are enhanced with oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups, such as –OH, –COOH, –COC–, and –NH2, non-covalent interaction can also occur simultaneously via hydrogen bonds. Moreover, the plasma treatment can induce chain entanglements of the unreacted chitosan, further improving the compressive properties of the NGQD composite. Similar mechanisms, therefore, can also be expected for the NGQD/AuNP composites. To elucidate the formation of hydrogen bonds in the composites, both composites containing NGQDs (Sample A) and AuNPs (Sample C) were cut and cross-connected as described in Fig. S5 c red box. By cross-connecting these two pieces together, the composite was able to self-heal in a short amount of time (20 min) (Fig. S5 c green box), suggesting the formation of hydrogen bonds induced by the chain dynamics of the composites. Video S2 provides a visual evidence on the self-healing composites.

The pore structures of the composites can be further engineered by freeze–thaw process (details in experimental section), in which physical cross-linking occurs through the formation of crystallites, liquid–liquid phase separation, and chain entanglements [45]. The presence of NGQDs/AuNPs can assist those processes to produce robust composite sponges (Fig. 3
a inset). Fig. 3 b presents the water storage capacities of different freeze-thawed composites. The freeze-thawed composites show improvements in the water storage ability up to ∼ 700 % for Sample A and ∼ 500 % for Sample B, C, and D compared with the chitosan control sample. This observation can be attributed to the presence of hydrophilic NGQDs and AuNPs in the composite which are able to enhance the wettability and subsequently leads to an elevated water uptake. Nonetheless, the water uptake shows a decreasing tendency as the gold salt ratio increases. It is suggested that the water uptake was reduced upon the formation of NGQD-AuNP complexes due to the consumption of free oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups on the NGQD surfaces (see absorption and PL spectra in Fig. 1 a & c, respectively). The formed complexes might also covalently and/or non-covalently interact with the polymeric chitosan, further suppressing the water uptake of the composite.

Fig. 3 a presents the compression test of all sponges after immersion in water for 10 min. All samples exhibit the same stress from 0 to 10 % strain, except for Sample A which shows higher stress due to the rigid porous structure caused by the NGQDs. As the strain increases, the compression forces the trapped air out of the composites, and simultaneously enhances the wettability throughout the whole composite volume. The sudden enhancement in the compressive stress of chitosan sponge after 10 % strain is attributed to structural rigidity caused by the low wettability with the absence of NGQDs and AuNPs, which agrees well with the water uptake results in Fig. 3 b.

The cycle compression test was further performed to study the mechanical stability of the prepared composites. The chitosan sponge shows a clear large difference between the first and second cycles while all NGQD/AuNP composites only show small differences. Moreover, the NGQD/AuNP composites exhibit no apparent change between the second and third cycles, yet the chitosan sample displays a noticeable change. The change in the first and second cycles for the composites can be due to the characteristic evolution from rigid to soft composites, caused by the air expulsion and water wetting. It is also imperative that the chitosan sample starts to show noticeable compressive stress after around 20 and 30 % strain during the second and third cycles, respectively, caused by thickness reduction. Whereas the composites already show a noticeable compressive stress directly at the beginning or after around 5 % strain, inferring the formation of mechanically stable 3D structures. These observations indicate the positive effect of the embedded nanoscale NGQDs/AuNPs in the macroscale chitosan matrix that leads to exceptional properties. Further details about the mechanical compression results can be seen in supporting information S4 and Figure S6 .

The ability of the composites to absorb water is closely related to the porous structure within their 3D architecture, which acts as microchannel for the water pathways. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed to visualize the porous structures within the composites. Evidently, the control chitosan sample exhibits arbitrary structures with only a few pores (Fig. 4
a), whilst uniform porous structures are detected in the NGQD/AuNP composites (Fig. 4 b-e). To confirm the effect of the plasma-induced cross-linking in the composites, two control studies were performed involving NGQD hydrogel cured and heated at room-temperature and 60 °C for 20 min, respectively. As shown in Figure S8 , both control groups show arbitrary structures with few pores. Moreover, the pores are larger than the plasma-treated NGQD composites ( Figure S8
a-b(3) and Fig. 4 b(2)), suggesting that the plasma treatment is critical to promote cross-linking in the composite. Based on these results, one concludes that both NGQDs/AuNPs and microplasma are essential in controlling the porous structures within the composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multifunctional water treatment applications with NGQD/AuNP composites and colloidal NGQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The metal ion sensing performance of fabricated composites were investigated by exploiting the NGQD-induced PL properties. The PL maps of the composites in Figure S9 suggest that Sample A has the brightest PL emission. Hence, Sample A was chosen to conduct the PL sensing for heavy metal ions under 420 nm excitation wavelength. Fig. 5
a shows the heavy metal ion selectivity result after measuring the ratio between the PL intensities of sample (I, immersion in heavy metal ion solutions) and blank (I0 , immersion in DI water). The result indicates that the fabricated NGQD composite exhibits good selectivity toward Hg2+. By immersing the NGQD composites in different concentration of Hg2+ solutions, the PL intensities of NGQD composites decreases with increasing concentration of Hg2+ ( Figure S10 ). As shown in Fig. 5 b, the change in the PL intensity ratios versus Hg2+ concentrations can be well-fitted with a Stern-Volmer equation, ranging from 10 to 100 μM with a limit of detection (LoD) of 2.2 μM estimated using three times standard deviation (3σ) rule. This result indicates that the NGQD composites can be used for rapid and sensitive detection of Hg2+.

We further explored the organic dye adsorption using different dyes including RB, MB, CV, MO, CR, and RBBR. The test was carried out with 20 ppm concentration for 2 h, and Sample A exhibits a good selectivity toward anionic dyes (i.e., MO, CR, and RBBR) (Fig. 5 c). This observation can be attributed to the presence of amine functional group of the chitosan matrix, endowing the composite with positive surface charge. Interestingly, among those anionic dyes, Sample A is particularly sensitive toward RBBR, showing almost 100 % removal efficiency after 2 h (Fig. 5 c and Figure S11 ). The selectivity of dye adsorption is governed not only by the interaction between the adsorbents and the dyes, but also the chemical structures of the dyes and adsorbents [3]. Since MO has only one negative surface charge and CR has the highest molecular weight, the dye adsorption activity of Sample A towards those dyes is lower than for RBBR (possesses two negative surface charges and larger molecular weight than MO but smaller than CR). In addition, enhanced RBBR adsorption for NGQD composite can be plausibly caused not only by the occurrence of additional hydrogen bond coming from –OH, –COOH, and –NH groups, but also through the π-π interaction between the benzene ring of NGQD and RBBR. Interestingly, the presence of AuNPs in the Sample B, C and D reduces the RBBR removal efficiency, yet increases the removal efficiency of CR instead. Since the size of the AuNPs is increasing continuously with increasing gold salt to chitosan ratio, it is possible that the pore size of the fabricated composites becomes larger (see Fig. 4 e(2)), which might be more suitable for larger dyes, such as CR. The adsorption kinetics and isotherms of Sample A for RBBR adsorption were further analyzed. The adsorption kinetics was studied by fitting the experimental data with a pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetics. Figure S12 and Table S1 indicate that the experimental data fits better to the pseudo-first-order, implying that the adsorption of RBBR using NGQD composites is mainly π-π induced physisorption promoted by the aromatic NGQDs in the composites. In addition, the isotherm studies on the RBBR adsorption reveal that the adsorption process follows the Freundlich model instead of the Langmuir model (Fig. 5 d), suggesting a heterogeneous and multilayer adsorption of RBBR on NGQD composite (Sample A) [52].

The catalytic properties of the NGQD/AuNP composites for organic pollutants reduction was additional studied by hydrogenation of 4-NP using NaBH4. Figure S13 shows the decreasing and increasing absorption peaks of phenolate at 400 nm and 4-AP at 297 nm, respectively, upon the immersion of Sample C into the mixture of 4-NP and NaBH4. The methodology of the use of NaBH4 in the reduction of 4-NP into 4-AP can be seen in the supporting information S5 and other literatures [53,54]. To evaluate the catalytic reduction performance, the conversion of 4-NP was investigated as first-order reaction kinetics and the result is summarized in Fig. 5 e. All the composites demonstrate catalytic reduction activity except for Sample A which contains only NGQDs, indicating that the catalytic activities of the composites are attained from the AuNPs. As the gold salt to chitosan ratio increases, the slope which corresponds to the reaction rate constant (k) is getting higher, suggesting the enhanced catalytic performance. However, when the ratio increases from 1.64 to 2.45, there is a reduction in the slopes from 0.022 to 0.0189 min−1. This result could be explained with the reduced surface area due to large particle size with broad distribution of AuNPs in Sample D (see Figure S4 bottom). In addition, the large pores of Sample D (Fig. 4 e(2)) may allow the small 4-NP molecules to easily diffuse out without any spatial hindrances, giving result to low surface adsorption prior to reaction. Since Sample C exhibits the best catalytic activity, we further explored the temperature effect on the catalytic reaction. Fig. 5 f depicts the improved conversion at high temperatures. The activation energy (Ea) of the catalytic reaction using Sample C was acquired by establishing an Arrhenius plot involving 1/T versus ln k (Fig. 5 f inset). We estimated the Ea from the slope in Fig. 5 f inset to be 25.29 kJ mol−1, which is comparable with the previous reports [6,55,56]. The 4-AP produced in the system can be purified through a sequence of purification steps and then be used for pharmaceutical purposes, such as the production of analgesic and antipyretic drugs (details can be seen in supporting information S6 ) [57,58].

Colloidal NGQDs can be collected and purified from the leftover solution after the composite preparation ( Figure S1 ). The purified NGQDs show stable PL properties and were further used for metal ion detection. It is apparent that under 370 nm excitation wavelength, the NGQDs exhibit superior sensitivity toward Cr(VI) detection (Fig. 6
a) and the PL emission is dramatically quenched at higher Cr(VI) concentrations (Fig. 6 b). By following the Stern-Volmer equation, a linear correlation can be established starting from Cr(VI) concentration of 1 – 100 μM with LoD as low as 61 nM using the 3σ rule, as shown in Fig. 6 c. It was found that Cr(VI) exhibits a strong absorption peak around 370 nm ( Figure S14 ). This finding can be exploited for maximizing the inner filter effect (IFE) quenching phenomenon of GQDs [26,59]. Fortunately, the NGQDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission, and thus the excitation can be fine-tuned at 370 nm to yield the most PL excitation spectral overlap with the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) ( Figure S14 ). The excitation-dependent properties of the NGQDs can be attributed to the existence of several defects attributed by the functional groups and N-dopants, supported by the observation of a d-band peak in the Raman spectrum ( Figure S15 ). Detailed discussion about Raman results can be seen in supporting information S7 . Table S2 summarizes the performance of as-fabricated graphene-metal composites for water monitoring and purification, while the comparison with other literatures can be seen in Table S3-S5 .

Organic-metal composites with controlled structures, tunable compositions, and multifunctional properties are useful materials for diverse applications. This work presented a simple and controllable engineering of multifunctional porous chitosan-based graphene-metal composites containing uniformly dispersed NGQDs and AuNPs in a 3D polymeric matrix using non-thermal microplasmas under ambient conditions. Notably,

(1) the absence of any additional toxic chemical cross-linkers in our method further underpins the versatility of the microplasma-based technologies for environmental-friendly, green, and sustainable advanced composite materials processing at ambient conditions;

(2) synergistic effect of NGQD and AuNP within the porous composites leads to the multifunctional properties for water monitoring and purification, including stable and sensitive PL for heavy metals detection, high adsorption capacity of organic molecules, and remarkable catalytic activity for organic pollutant degradation;

(3) besides producing multifunctional composites, our system also yields colloidal NGQDs as side product which can also be used for other heavy metal ion sensing. Importantly, no precursor material is wasted, because the precursor is fully converted into both composites and colloidal NGQDs. This work offers a promising, environmental-friendly, and sustainable approach based on microplasma technologies to convert a naturally available biomass into advanced multifunctional materials for environment related applications. It is envisaged that our strategy is applicable for other nanomaterials including metal and inorganic NPs, semiconductor QDs, and metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) for various emerging applications.
